othermeta.name: description
othermeta.content: Definitions for key terms used in markdown-validator documentation: front matter, rule, rule set, flag, operator, workflow, XPath, and more.

glossentry.glossdef: A validation rule that checks the content of the Markdown document below the front-matter block. Body rules use XPath expressions to query the rendered HTML. Contrast with header rule.

Continuous Integration / Continuous Delivery. An automated pipeline that builds, tests, and validates code and content on every pull request. markdown-validator runs as a step in a CI/CD pipeline to gate merges on validation results.

An integer returned by a command-line program when it finishes. markdown-validator exits with 0 when all Required rules pass and 1 when any Required rule fails. CI systems use exit codes to determine whether a step succeeded or failed.

A field in a validation rule that controls how the query result is processed before being compared to the expected value. Header flags include value, check, date, and pattern. Body flags include count, text, dom, and all. See also: operator.

A YAML metadata block at the top of a Markdown file, delimited by --- lines. Front matter contains structured metadata such as title, ms.topic, ms.date, and description. markdown-validator requires every file to have a front-matter block.
A validation rule that checks the YAML front-matter metadata at the top of a Markdown file. Header rules use a metadata key name as their query field. Contrast with body rule.

A plain-English remediation message shown to an author when a rule fails. Defined in the rule's mitigation field. A good mitigation message is an imperative sentence that tells the author exactly what to change.

A comparison function applied between the processed query result and the expected value in a rule. Operators include equality (==), inequality (!=), contains ([]), starts-with ([:), regex match (r), and length limit (l). See also: flag.

An error raised when markdown-validator cannot read a .md file because it does not begin with a valid YAML front-matter block. A file that produces a ParseError is counted as a failed scan and contributes to the exit code of 1.

Read–Eval–Print Loop. An interactive command-line session started with md-validate repl. The REPL lets rule creators load a Markdown file, inspect its structure, test XPath expressions, and evaluate rule JSON objects before committing rules to a rule set file.

A JSON object that defines a single assertion about a Markdown document. A rule specifies what to query (query), how to process the result (flag), how to compare it (operation), and what the expected value is (value). Rules have a severity level (Required or Suggested) and a mitigation message.

A JSON file that defines all the validation rules and workflows for a specific content type. A rule set has three top-level elements: rules.header, rules.body, and workflows. Rule set files are version-controlled in the documentation repository and used by both local validation and CI.

The output produced by validating one Markdown file against a rule set. A scan report includes a score (passing rules / total rules), a per-rule pass/fail result, and mitigation messages for each failure. Available in text, JSON, and CSV formats.

The number of rules that passed divided by the total number of rules evaluated, expressed as a fraction (for example, 24/26). A score of 26/26 means every rule passed. The score does not directly determine the CI exit code — only the pass/fail status of Required rules does.

A rule severity level that indicates a best-practice recommendation. Suggested-level failures are reported but do not affect the CLI exit code. A CI pipeline step passes even if Suggested rules fail. Contrast with Required.

A rule severity level that indicates a mandatory editorial standard. Required-level failures set the CLI exit code to 1, causing a CI pipeline step to fail and blocking the merge. The default level if level is omitted from a rule. Contrast with Suggested.

A named sequence of rule evaluation steps that uses conditional branching. Workflows allow a check to run only when a precondition is met. Defined in the workflows array of a rule set file using the workflow step language.

XML Path Language. A query language for selecting nodes in an XML or HTML document. markdown-validator body rules use XPath 1.0 expressions to query the rendered HTML of a Markdown file's body. The HTML is flat: all headings are direct children of <body>.
